coredata.dc:title: Biological assessment of synthesized carbon dots from polyphenol enriched extract of pomegranate peel, incorporated with Mentha piperita essential oil
coredata.dc:creator: Sattariazar, Simin
Arsalani, Nasser
Nejad Ebrahimi, Samad
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention of many researchers in terms of their extraordinary properties as a new and progressive science in nanomedicine. Pomegranate peel is an agricultural waste due to its chemical feature and diverse bioactive compounds with many health benefits. This work used a polyphenol-enriched pomegranate peel extract (PPE) as a precursor to synthesize CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal process. M. piperita essential oil (MEO) as a drug model loaded on CDs with the hydrothermal method. In addition, organic additives such as ethylene diamine, urea, and citric acid were used to adjust the fluorescence emission. Acquired carbon dots were characterized using UV–Vis, ATR-FTIR, PL, DLS, FESEM, HRTEM, EDAX, and Zeta potential analysis. Hence, the prepared carbon dots’ biological efficiency was studied via various in vitro tests, for instance, antioxidant, antibacterial, and cytotoxicity assays. The Korsmeyer- Peppas kinetic model governed the release behavior of CDs and CDs/MEO. The experiment confirmed that carbon dots (CDs, CDs/MEO) distributing the desired and uniform sizes of 6.1 ± 1.2 nm and 33.09 ± 5.1, respectively, exhibited bright blue color with a quantum yield of 20.51% and 17. 50% belongs to CDs and CDs/MEO, respectively. The developed CDs and CDs/MEO were tested for in brine shrimp assay and no toxicity was observed. Furthermore, the Gram-positive bacteria staphylococcus aureus was eradicated by CDs/MEO with a minimum inhibition concentration (MIC) of 0.31 mg ml−1. Antioxidant scavenging activity was investigated in the DPPH assay. Moreover, CDs with IC50 = 22.18 μg ml−1 have more antioxidant activity. Indeed, the results show obvious enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial activity, which opens a new way for extensive applications of carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyphenol enriched
Biological activity
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis and PL analysis with different kinds of surfactants, HPLC analysis
In vitro active substance release studies of CDs and CDs/MEO

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministère de l’Éducation, Gouvernement de l’Ontario

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Fig. 4 displays the size, morphology, and element mapping of prepared CDs and CDs/MEO. TEM, FESEM, and DLS analysis determined the actual shape and size of the CDs. TEM analysis (A) and (C) results indicated that the CDs and CDs/MEO shapes were spherical, with uniform and an average size of 6 ± 1.3 nm and 33.09 ± 5.1 belonged to CDs and CDs/MEO, respectively. More TEM images of the CDs/MEO sample with different magnifications have been attached in supporting information in S2 section. The size of CDs, which have established a covalent bond with mint essential oil, was grown. However, there was no significant change in fluorescence emission. FESEM data analysis (E) indicated that the average size of CDs was 27.35 nm. The elemental distribution of C, N, and O in the synthesized CDs was investigated by EDS analysis (energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy). Based on the data shown in Fig. 4 (F), the synthesized CDs consist of C, O, and N. The average hydrodynamic size of CDs was measured by DLS. the hydrodynamic size of CDs is 10–100 nm with PdI = 0.539. Furthermore, the statistics graph verified this. The stability of synthesized nanoparticles was evaluated by measuring the zeta potential (ZP). The average ZP of CDs was (−9) mV at pH = 7.4. This amount indicates CDs have good stability toward aggregation. The changes in ZP value from (−9) to (−12) mV indicate that the essential oil of M. piperita is loaded onto CDs.The University of Tabriz financially supported this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption of CDs with three kinds of surfactant (EDA, CA, Urea). (B) Absorption and Fluorescence emission of CDs/MEO at 360 nm excitation. (C) several emission wavelengths of CDs/MEO from 340 to 440 nm. (D) different emission wavelengths of CDs from 340 to 440 nm.
HPLC chromatogram of polyphenol enriched pomegranate peel extract recorded at 254 nm.
ATR-FTIR (attenuated total reflection) spectra of PPE (a), CDs (b), CDs/MEO (c), Menthol (d).

(A) TEM image of CDs (B) Histogram of the Particle Size Distribution of CDs (C) TEM image of CDs/MEO (D) Histogram of particle Size Distribution of CDs/MEO (E) FESEM micrograph of CDs (F) EDS Analysis plot of CDs (G) Size Distribution of CDs (H) Size Statistic graph of CDs.
in vitro release behaviour from pH-sensitive nanocarrier (CDs) (a), and (CDs/MEO) (b).
The schematic illustration preparation of the fluorescent CDs.
Evaluation of MIC (mg.ml−1) against bacterial strains.
Mortality percent of Artemia to assess the toxicity of CDs and CDs/MEO.
Parameters and correlation coefficient (Korsmeyer kinetics) were applied to the experimental release profile.

head.ce:title: Biological assessment of synthesized carbon dots from polyphenol enriched extract of pomegranate peel, incorporated with Mentha piperita essential oil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention of many researchers in terms of their extraordinary properties as a new and progressive science in nanomedicine. Pomegranate peel is an agricultural waste due to its chemical feature and diverse bioactive compounds with many health benefits. This work used a polyphenol-enriched pomegranate peel extract (PPE) as a precursor to synthesize CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal process. M. piperita essential oil (MEO) as a drug model loaded on CDs with the hydrothermal method. In addition, organic additives such as ethylene diamine, urea, and citric acid were used to adjust the fluorescence emission. Acquired carbon dots were characterized using UV–Vis, ATR-FTIR, PL, DLS, FESEM, HRTEM, EDAX, and Zeta potential analysis. Hence, the prepared carbon dots’ biological efficiency was studied via various in vitro tests, for instance, antioxidant, antibacterial, and cytotoxicity assays. The Korsmeyer- Peppas kinetic model governed the release behavior of CDs and CDs/MEO. The experiment confirmed that carbon dots (CDs, CDs/MEO) distributing the desired and uniform sizes of 6.1 ± 1.2 nm and 33.09 ± 5.1, respectively, exhibited bright blue color with a quantum yield of 20.51% and 17. 50% belongs to CDs and CDs/MEO, respectively. The developed CDs and CDs/MEO were tested for in brine shrimp assay and no toxicity was observed. Furthermore, the Gram-positive bacteria staphylococcus aureus was eradicated by CDs/MEO with a minimum inhibition concentration (MIC) of 0.31 mg ml−1. Antioxidant scavenging activity was investigated in the DPPH assay. Moreover, CDs with IC50 = 22.18 μg ml−1 have more antioxidant activity. Indeed, the results show obvious enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial activity, which opens a new way for extensive applications of carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last few years, the study of carbon dots as a new group of zero-dimensional nanoparticles has been widely practiced. They have various applications in various fields due to their extraordinary properties, including biocompatibility, low toxicity, water solubility, and high quantum yield. Because of its advantages, such as being functionalized for specific purposes and being easy to summarize, it has become a target for many research groups [1,2]. Carbon dots are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with the attachment of surface functional groups (OH and COOH), which can be doped with N, S, and P heteroatoms; thus, their tunable characteristics and fluorescence emission are highly useful in biomedical applications such as bioimaging, photodynamic therapy, biosensing and drug delivery [3–6].

The choice of carbon precursor is one of the most significant parameters in preparing carbon dots. Because of its effect on application and reaction process, recently, natural provenances as a novel group of precursors were hailed by researchers to develop carbon dots. Specifically, during the past decade, waste material, chiefly bio-waste, has become a remarkable carbon precursor for carbon dots generation due to its advantages, such as being economically viable, easily accessible, good biocompatibility, and mitigating the destructive effects on the environment [7–9]. Furthermore, by doing so, worthless materials are turned into valuable materials with various uses. Some waste sources for the carbon dots precursor are eggshell [10], rice husks [11], willow bark [12], lemon peels [13], bamboo leaves [14], tea leaves [15,16], grass [17], pomelo peel [18], potato starch [19] and coriander leaves [20].

Studies show organic compounds can be converted to carbon dots via carbonization. However, the mechanism of this reaction is still unclear. Natural carbon dots can be prepared in two ways: top-down and bottom-up, sometimes a combination of two systems, but in any case, the top-down method is preferable. In the ״top-down״ process, carbon sources include waste, biomass, and fruit [21,22]. The bottom-up approach is suitable for polymer compounds. Molecular aggregation is a bottom-up method despite the top-down process, which requires growing from seed to carbon dots such as chitin and chitosan [22,23]. Top-down approaches include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave chemistry, and extraction [24,25]. Hydrothermal technology is a robust procedure in developing nanomaterials with high biocompatibility, high efficiency, optimal controllability, and preparing production with a uniform size distribution, less environmental damage, and superior features method. Hydrothermal is a technique based on the formation and growth of crystals due to the chemical reactions (homogeneous and heterogeneous) and changes in the solubility of materials in an aqueous solution under appropriate temperature and pressure [26,27]. As usual, plant and fruit extracts contain multiple bioactive substances such as polyphenol, tannin, citric acid, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, etc., which become fascinating when synthesizing the nanoparticles [28,29].

Pomegranate peel contains 38% of the total weight of fruit and is a rich source of polyphenolic compounds. Pomegranate peels possess various phenolic compounds, including hydrolysable tannins, hydroxybenzoic acid, anthocyanins, and ellagitannins. Among these, ellagitannins are the conquering phenolic species and punicalagin is the major compound present [30–32]. Pomegranate peels also offer an untapped source of phenolic compounds called antioxidant properties. This study applied to the development CDs based on natural precursors (PPE, M. piperita) with least toxicity and compared their antioxidant and antibacterial potential before and after converting to carbon dots. We expect the biological performance of the newly acquired carbon dots to be enhanced and were hoped to inherit the advantages of their precursors. Also, the actual active substance release profile from CDs and CDs/MEO has been represented by using Korsmeyer- Pippa's kinetic model. Results also revealed that our expectations have been to a large extent.

Ethylenediamine, Urea, Citric acid, and polyethylene glycol (PEG- 1000) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and Merck Co. Waste pomegranate peels were received from a beverage company. 2,2-diphenyl-1- picrylhydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH) free radical, punicalagin, and ellagic acid as Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Quinine purchased standards was purchased by Merck Chemical Co. the polystyrene adsorption resin (Diaion® HP-20) from Mitsubishi Chemical (Tokyo, Japan). Deionized water applied throughout the experiment was obtained from the ultrapure water purification system, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol, and sulfuric acid 96% from Sigma-Aldrich. Pure Artemia Cysts from the Great Salt Lake. M. piperita Essential Oil (MEO) from Vasha Darou Pars Herbal Pharmaceutical company (Qom, Iran).

Carbon dots are synthesized with two steps: 1) preparation of enriched extract of polyphenol compounds from pomegranate peel (PPE), 2) synthesis of CDs from enriched extract with a one-step hydrothermal method. At first, pomegranate peels were washed with water and then dried in natural light, then, dried pomegranate peels were ground and put (500 g) in ethanol-water (80/20 v/v) for three days. The resulting extract was concentrated using a rotary apparatus. The resinous substance left over from the evaporation of the solvent is dissolved (120 g) in the deionized water and then loaded on Diaion HP-20 resin column (Mitsubishi Chemical, Tokyo, Japan) (70 cm
×
8 cm); ethanol fraction was obtained after washed with water and ethanol and finally, it was evaporated and lyophilized to produce an enriched extract (PPE). The procured extract was analyzed with HPLC. Briefly, 0.5 g of enriched extraction, 50 ml deionized water, added (0.3 g Urea, 0.3 ml EDA, 0.3gr CA), and 0.3g PEG 1000 were mixed. The sample was stirred for about 15 min to create a homogeneous solution. Then the model was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at (150, 170, 180
) °C
for (2, 4, 6, 8h); as shown in (Scheme 1 ) CDs prepared with ethylenediamine have higher quantum yield 20.51%. According to the data analyses, water as a solvent and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source to regulate fluorescence emission, temperature, and time reaction 180 °C 8h, respectively, were considered the optimal conditions. Eventually, the autoclave cools down naturally at room temperature. The supernatant was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. After the purification, after centrifuging, CDs were purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 14 KDa) for one day in the dark against distilled water (dialysate was replaced three times at 8h intervals).

CDs/MEO feature, via the following method, was prepared. Around 1 ml of Mentha piperita essential oil was added to 20 ml of CDs (2 mg ml−1) solution, transferred to stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 180
°C
for 4 h. The supernatant solution was centrifuged, and unreacted particles were collected. The brown solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag of 14000 Da for 8 h. For evaluating the loading efficiency of M. piperita essential oil on CDs, the outer solution of the dialysis bag (unloaded Mint) was analyzed with UV–Vis absorption at 220 nm and then concentration was calculated with the following equation (1):
loading
amount =
(
m 0
−
m 1
)
m 0
× 100
Where
m 0
is the total amount of M. piperita essential oil and
m 1
is the unloaded M. piperita essential oil.

Ultraviolet–vis (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were registered on (Shimadzu Analytic Jena), and fluorescence spectra were carried out on (PerkinElmer) Fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy was used to study the properties of materials on the nanoscale (TEM). The surface morphology and elemental analysis (EDAX) of CDs samples were performed by field emission scattering electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (MIRA3 TESCAN). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) equipped with ATR recorded on (THEMO NICOLET). To determine the size distribution of CDs and Zeta-Potential Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer-ZS90, Malvern, UK) was used. The chemical composition of MEO was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC) using a Thermoquset- Finnigan Trace GC devise with a DB-5 Fused-silica cap, column (30 m*0.25 mm*0.25
μ
m) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID). Identifying the phenolic compounds of PPE carried out by Waters, 2695 Alliance HPLC System equipped with 966 Photo-diode Array Detector (PDA) using reverse-phase Column (Waters Sunfire C18 Column, 100 Å, 3.5
μ m
, 4.6 mm
×
150 mm) was used.

As mentioned above, the quantum yield (
Q Y
) of CDs with different surfactants according was measured by a reference process (a reference to quinine sulfate,
Q Y
= 0.564) according to the following equation (2) [33,34].
Q Y =
Q Y
s t
.
F .
A
s t
.
n 2
F
s t
. A .
n
s t
2
where Q is the quantum yield, F is the fluorescent area, A is the absorbance, and n is the refraction index. CDs with high quantum yield were selected to prepare CDs/MEO.

The release treatment of CDs and CDs/MEO was done separately; 2 ml (1 mg ml−1) were transferred into a dialysis bag with (MWCO 14 KDa) and then immersed into 40 ml buffer solution at various pH = 7.4, 4.5, 1.2 [35,36]. The release system stirred gently in the constant temperature simulated physiological ambiance (37
°C
). The release of active constituents from CDs and CDs/MEO was investigated by the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic model [37]. The calibration curve of CDs and CDs/MEO were
y = 0.0476 x + 0.0421
and
y = 0.2568 x − 0.148
with correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9975 and 0.998 respectively. This model includes several release mechanisms simultaneously, such as swelling the matrix and diffusion of the water into the matrix. The rate of active compounds released from CDs and CDs/MEO, as a function of time, was arranged based on the Korsmeyer-Peppas model by equation (3) [38].
M t
M ∞
=
K K
t n

M t
M ∞
indicates the release rate of active ingredient at time t, kk is the Korsmeyer constant, and n is a diffusion exponent, which determines the release mechanism of CDs and CDs/MEO; drug release follows Fick law (Case I) when n < 0.43, Drug release is considered eccentric, which includes a combination of diffusion and swelling release when 0.43 < n < 0.85, if n = 0.85, the release of the drug follows zero-order (Case II).

To evaluate the scavenging abilities of CDs and CDs/MEO the DPPH method was performed [39,40]. DPPH (2,2 phenyl-1- picrylhydrazyl) radical is a free and stable radical with high absorption at 517 nm with a single electron; antioxidants can pair with this single electron by losing hydrogen. This reaction causes the solution's DPPH reagent color to change from dark pink to yellow. This color change is measurable. This procedure calculates antioxidant capacity by the following formula (4) (Inhibition %).
I n h i b i t i o n
% =
A c − A s
A c
× 100

Hence, Ac (absorbance of control) has all the chemicals except samples, As (absorbance of the sample). This test process is as follows: 4 different volumes (5,10, 15, 20, 25) μl were transferred from stock 1000 μg ml−1 of each sample to a 96-house plate, respectively, after which methanol and DPPH were added in specified amounts. The samples were incubated in the dark at room temperature for 1 h the absorption of each well was read and recorded at 517 nm by Elisa Microplate Readers. Gallic acid was used as the standard.

The antibacterial activity was carried out according to the presented standards by (CLSI) Clinical Laboratory Standard Institute. Bacteria under study: Staphylococcus aureus ATCC (1431), Escherichia coli PTCC (1399), moreover Cefixime was evaluated as a standard antibiotic. Specific concentration from samples was prepared, and MIC (Minimum Inhibitory Concentration) was measured [41].

Cellular biocompatibility was evaluated using Artemia Brine Shrimp Lethality Test (BST) [42]. Therefore, to accomplish this test initially, artemia sist was hatched for 30 h. Hatched larvae separated and ten of them were transferred into plate wells that contained 1 ml of artificial seawater. Finally, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μg ml−1 of CDs and CDs/MEO were dropped in each column and the first column was considered the control column. DMSO was considered a positive control. After 24h, the percentage mortality of Artemia was calculated using the following equation (5):
Mortality
( % )
=
⌊
(
m − M
)
/ S
⌋
× 100
where m is the average number of dead larvae in samples, M is the average number of dead larvae in the control column, and S is the average number of live larvae in the control columns. This test was performed as three independent tests and the results were reported as Mean ± SD.

To study the profile of the enriched extract of pomegranate peel, reverse phase chromatography with eluting gradient technique was used, the mobile phase including (A) water and (B) methanol solvents (containing 0.02% trifluoroacetic acid). The eluting process was 98% A in 5 min, 50% A in 30 min, 100% B in 35 min, and 100% B until 38 min, then to 98% A in 40 min, 98%A in 42 min. The injection volume was 20
μ l
and the corresponding chromatogram was obtained at 254 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and PL analysis with different kinds of surfactants, HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 illustrates UV–Vis absorption spectrum (A) from 200 nm to 800 nm; UV–Vis spectrum A of CDs with various surfactants display two peaks at 289 nm and 387 nm, which is related to
π →
π *
transition and
n →
π *
, respectively. The first absorption is attributed to the CC band; the second, weaker peak is related to the CO band. (B) shows the PL emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption of CDs/MEO. (C) and (D) display different emission wavelengths corresponding with the change of excitation wavelengths of CDs/MEO and CDs, respectively, which indicates that fluorescent emission is adjustable by excitation wavelength. PL emission intensity decreased simultaneously with increased excitation wavelength; this phenomenon confirms particle size uniformity in synthesized CDs and CDs/MEO and could be related to various energy levels associated with different functional groups on carbon dots surface [43].

The hydroalcoholic extract of pomegranate peel was enriched with an application of HP-20 Diaion resin. The phytochemical profile of PPE analyzed by HPLC-UV and PPE chromatogram at 254 nm indicates in Fig. 2
. The HPLC results identified three major compounds, namely punicalagin-
α
, punicalagin-
β
, and ellagic acid. These results correspond with those published data [44–46].

The chemical composition of M. piperita essential oil was analyzed by GC-MS and GC-FID analysis, the data represented in Table S1. According to GC-FID results, eighteen compounds were identified. Two main ingredients were menthol (34.6%) and menthone (19.2%), which significantly contribute to biological activities, especially antimicrobial activities. The obtained results corresponded with previously reported data [47].

Fig. 3 . Shows the FT-IR spectrum of (PPE, CDs, CDs/MEO, and Menthol); by comparing the spectra with each other, we see structural changes while preparing CDs and CDs/MEO; these changes indicate that CDs’ surface has changed during the hydrothermal process through
π
interaction, electrostatic attraction and probably covalent bond. The first spectrum (a), which belongs to the enriched extract absorption bond at 3347 cm-1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H, and the resonance at 1583 cm-1 an expression stretching vibration of CO. The second spectrum (b) belongs to the CDs, the broad bonds at 3244 cm-1 to 2929 cm-1 are attributed to stretching vibrations O-H and N-H, and the stretching vibration of C-H, the stretching vibration of the CO group appeared at 1661 cm-1. Peak related to stretching vibration CC be revealed at 1567 cm-1. Besides, the resonance at 1353 cm-1 belonged to C-O, strengthening the presence of carboxylic acid groups. Comparing two spectra related to CDs and CDs/MEO (c) we find that the major groups in CDs are present in the CDs/MEO structure, as can be seen from the menthol spectrum (d), the peak in 3243 cm-1, which is related to the alcoholic OH and two other peaks
removed in the CDs/MEO spectrum, indicating the hydroxyl group of menthol, the main compound of M. piperita essential oils, is conjugated with functional groups of CDs.

Fig. 4 displays the size, morphology, and element mapping of prepared CDs and CDs/MEO. TEM, FESEM, and DLS analysis determined the actual shape and size of the CDs. TEM analysis (A) and (C) results indicated that the CDs and CDs/MEO shapes were spherical, with uniform and an average size of 6 ± 1.3 nm and 33.09 ± 5.1 belonged to CDs and CDs/MEO, respectively. More TEM images of the CDs/MEO sample with different magnifications have been attached in supporting information in S2 section. The size of CDs, which have established a covalent bond with mint essential oil, was grown. However, there was no significant change in fluorescence emission. FESEM data analysis (E) indicated that the average size of CDs was 27.35 nm. The elemental distribution of C, N, and O in the synthesized CDs was investigated by EDS analysis (energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy). Based on the data shown in Fig. 4 (F), the synthesized CDs consist of C, O, and N. The average hydrodynamic size of CDs was measured by DLS. the hydrodynamic size of CDs is 10–100 nm with PdI = 0.539. Furthermore, the statistics graph verified this. The stability of synthesized nanoparticles was evaluated by measuring the zeta potential (ZP). The average ZP of CDs was (−9) mV at pH = 7.4. This amount indicates CDs have good stability toward aggregation. The changes in ZP value from (−9) to (−12) mV indicate that the essential oil of M. piperita is loaded onto CDs.

Table 1 shows the MIC values for (PPE), MEO, CDs, and CDs/MEO against S. aureus and E. coli bacterial strains. The results show that the CDs/MEO sample has the highest inhibitory effect on the growth of S. aureus bacteria at 0.31 mg ml−1 a concentration, while PPE extract prevented the E. coli bacteria grows at a concentration of 0.62 mg ml−1. CDs at the same concentration showed a MIC value of 1.25 mg/ml versus S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. Comparing the MIC data, we conclude that CDs/MEO has a strong inhibitory effect against S. aureus compared to other cases, which are affected by M. piperita coupling with CDs.

Table 2 shows the percent mortality amount of Artemia, which was placed at room temperature overnight in the presence of different concentrations (10, 15, 20, 25) μg.ml−1 of CDs and CDs/MEO. The results display that the toxicity of CDs and CDs/MEO is not high, mainly in low concentration, compared to DMSO as a positive control. The results of the test confirm that the CDs are non-toxic. Considering the antioxidant and antibacterial properties of the prepared CDs and CDs/MEO, it can be concluded that they are not toxic to normal cells.

Table 3 indicates the result of radical scavenging activities in DPPH assays. The IC50 values were indicated for each sample and based on that, the antioxidant power of samples compared with each other and with Gallic acid as a standard. The result displays that CDs and PPE have more potent antioxidant activity than Gallic acid. It is noticeable that CDs inhibitory power is significantly reduced after coupling with M. piperita essential oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro active substance release studies of CDs and CDs/MEO

ce:section.ce:para: The present study investigated the biological properties of synthesized CDs from pomegranate peel polyphenols before and after binding to M. piperita essential oil. The results were compared with the properties of the extract. Factors such as the type of precursor, particle size, surface functional groups, and additives containing heteroatoms such as urea, citric acid, and ethylenediamine can affect quantum efficiency [48,49]. Due to the unpair electrons present on the surface, carbon dots have a significant antioxidant potential through scavenging free radicals [50]. In this study, CDs prepared from pomegranate peel polyphenols with IC50 = 22.18 (μg ml−1) had a higher inhibitory effect compared to PPE, CDs/MEO, and also from alginic acid used as standard. The antibacterial activity exhibited that carbon dots modified with M. piperita essential oil had a more inhibitory effect on S. aureus bacteria in comparison to M. piperita essential oil, PPE, and CDs. The results showed that despite the presence of a thick layer of peptido-glycan in the Gram-positive bacteria, Staphylococcus has a more effective function than Escherichia coli. Many reports of carbon dots antimicrobial studies [51]. Carbon dots are non-toxic due to their high biocompatibility [52]. in this research, the toxicity of synthesized carbon dots was investigated by (BST) test, and the results confirmed that the prepared carbon dots had no toxicity, at least against normal cells. From the discovery of carbon dots until now, drug delivery has been one of the most important applications of carbon dots [53,54]. In this project, the release of the active substance of carbon dots was investigated by Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic model at three various pH. The results reveal that the release mechanism in both carbon dots (CDs, CDs/MEO) follows Fick's law in all three pHs except pH = 4.5 in CDs.

In the current research, we produced CDs from the polyphenol-enriched pomegranate peel extract, particularly first-time. These products have fantastic physicochemical and biological properties, for example, high fluorescence stability, water-solubility, and biocompatibility. Furthermore, developed CDs conjugated with M. piperita essential oil gave it unique properties without any significant change in fluorescent emission. Biological studies showed antibacterial properties of M. piperita against S. aureus bacteria improved after conjugation with CDs. Also, the antioxidant evaluation showed that CDs IC50 = 22.18 (μg ml−1) alone are more vital antioxidants than PPE IC50 = 33.08 (μg ml−1) and Gallic acid IC50 = 40.11 (μg ml−1). Finally, releasing the active ingredients from the prepared nanoparticles indicates that they are pH-sensitive nanocarriers. Outcomes demonstrate that these Carbon dots have great potential in biomedical, bioimaging, and drug delivery.

Simin Sattariazar: experimented with and developed the first draft of the manuscript. Nasser Arsalani: supervised the project and proofreading of the manuscript and. Samad Nejad Ebrahimi: supervised the project and proofreading of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The University of Tabriz financially supported this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126981.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biogreen synthesis of carbon dots for biotechnology and nanomedicine applications
Nano-Micro Lett.
